front: Film: knight named Antonius Block plays chess with Death during the Black Death; ends with a Danse Macabre on a hill
Film: knight Antonius Block is played by Max von Sydow; set during the plague as he returns from the Crusades
Film: ends with cast parading in silhouette on a hill, pulled along by a shrouded figure with a scythe
Film: Jons saves a girl from being raped by Raval
Film: success at Cannes provided funds for the production of The Seventh Seal
back: Smiles of a Summer Night (Ingmar Bergman)

front: Film: a Scott Walker song titled after this film recounts 'this morning I played chess with Death, said the knight'
Film: nurse Alma attends to mute actress Elisabet Vogler; their faces merge together using smoke and mirrors in a single close-up
Film: after being threatened with boiling water, the mute actress finally begins to speak to her nurse
Film: near the end, Elisabet Vogler breaks her silence by saying 'nothing' (ingenting) when commanded by Alma
Film: opens with a crawling spider, a nail driven into a hand, then a morgue where a boy wakes when a phone rings
Film: Susan Sontag noted the 'calculated frustration' of the ending — a woman boarding a bus alone
Film: Professor Isak Borg journeys to Lund to receive his Doctor Jubilaris degree, picking up hitchhikers who inspire memories and nightmares
Film: dream sequence features clocks without hands; ends with a man dragged into a coffin by his own corpse
Film: final screen performance of Victor Sjostrom (director of The Phantom Carriage), who plays the elderly professor
Film: Isak Borg is told he is 'guilty of guilt'
Film: Charlotte gives daughter Eva a car and wristwatch after listening to her play Chopin's Prelude No. 2 in A Minor
Film: Karin writhes before a window overlooking the sea after hearing voices from the wallpaper; has a vision of God as a malevolent spider
Film: at the end, a writer (David) tells his son at a window that love and God might be one and the same
Film: features the last of four versions of the saraband from J.S. Bach's Cello Suite No. 2
Film: ends with a Russian roulette scene — the revolver is revealed to contain soot instead of bullets
back: Smiles of a Summer Night (Ingmar Bergman)

front: Film: inspired the Stephen Sondheim musical A Little Night Music, which includes 'Send in the Clowns'
back: Smiles of a Summer Night (Ingmar Bergman)

front: Film: a priest comforts a fisherman afraid of nuclear war; Bergman compared it to 'chamber music'; inspired Paul Schrader's First Reformed
Film: boy named Ismael foretells an old woman knocking over a lamp, causing a fire that kills the protagonist's abusive step-father
Film: two sisters — the sensual Ester and her sickly intellectual sister Anna; opens on a train where Johan watches tanks pass; a boy reads A Hero of Our Time
Director: longtime cinematographer was Sven Nykvist, who later worked with Tarkovsky on The Sacrifice and with Louis Malle on Black Moon
Director: Andrei Tarkovsky frequently mimicked his use of sparse sound and later hired his cinematographer for The Sacrifice
Director: lived and worked on the island of Faro; inspired Mia Hansen-Love's 2021 film Bergman Island
Director: the band Sparks wrote a radio musical imagining his 'seduction' — magical transportation to 1950s Hollywood
Director: in Woody Allen's Manhattan, Mary upsets Isaac by placing this director in her 'Academy of the Overrated'
Director: as a child staged operas in a toy marionette theatre; later directed a TV adaptation of Mozart's The Magic Flute
Director: a New Order B-side to 'Everything's Gone Green' titled 'Cries and Whispers' was named after one of his films
